entry.title: Unable to connect Eclipse BW Modeling Tools / ABAP Development Tools to SAP BW/4HANA 2023 Trial on S
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I deployed a SAP BW/4HANA 2023 SP04 Trial appliance through SAP Cloud Appliance Library (CAL) on Microsoft Azure and am having trouble connecting Eclipse BW Modeling Tools and ABAP Development Tools (ADT).

Environment

  • SAP BW/4HANA 2023 SP04 Trial Appliance

  • Deployed via SAP CAL on Microsoft Azure

  • Eclipse with:

    • BW Modeling Tools

    • ABAP Development Tools (ADT)

  • Accessing from a local Windows workstation

What I Have Verified

  • Appliance status is Active in CAL.

  • CAL provides SAP GUI connection information:

    • SID: A4H

    • Instance Number: 00

    • Client: 100

  • Network connectivity:

    • Port 3200 reachable

    • Port 44300 reachable

  • ADT endpoint responds:

    • https://<server>:44300/sap/bc/adt

    • Returns 403 Forbidden, which suggests the ICF service is responding.

Eclipse Configuration

For both BW Project and ABAP Project creation:

  • Connection Type: Custom Application Server

  • System ID: A4H

  • Application Server: <public hostname/IP>

  • Instance Number: 00

Problem

Project creation fails before user authentication with:

Logon to system A4H failed
(reject connection request from <local_machine>:eclipse to <server>:sapdp00)

The error appears before any username/password validation.

Additional Information

  • CAL "Connect" only downloads a .sap file for SAP GUI.

  • SAP GUI is not yet installed, so I have not been able to verify a successful SAP GUI logon.

  • The same error occurs regardless of the SAP user entered.

Questions

  1. What does the error

    reject connection request ... sapdp00

    typically indicate in a BW/4HANA Trial appliance?

  2. Are there SAP profile parameters, dispatcher settings, gateway settings, or network restrictions that must be adjusted for Eclipse BW Modeling Tools / ADT access?

  3. Is connecting directly through the public Azure VM IP/hostname supported for this appliance, or should Eclipse use an internal hostname configured within the SAP system?

  4. Has anyone successfully connected Eclipse BW Modeling Tools or ADT to the BW/4HANA 2023 Trial appliance from CAL? If so, what connection type and hostname configuration were used?

  5. Would a successful SAP GUI logon be expected before Eclipse connectivity can work, or are there known scenarios where SAP GUI works but ADT/BWMT does not?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Incorrect Planned Order Quantity Calculation in MD11 Based on Scrap % and lot size procedure
entry.content:
The planned order created using transaction code MD11 shows an incorrect increase in order quantity based on the scrap percentage maintained in the MRP1 view of the Material Master.

Given that a fixed lot size is maintained for the material, the expectation is that the system should adjust the order quantity against the scrap percentage accordingly, without inflating the total quantity.

We observe the correct behavior when creating process orders and when planned orders are generated through the MRP/MPS run. The same behavior is expected for manually created planned orders as well.

Please find the attachment with details of test data and screenshots. Kindly look in to the issue and advise as it is impacting our project where the planned orders are created externally and posted to SAP.

entry.title: A Big Leap Beyond C4C: Changes in Communication Systems & Authentication in Sales Cloud V2 - Part 1
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog explores the changes to the Communication System in SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2 (SSCV2) that lay the foundation for integrating with external systems, whether through direct API connectivity, middleware-mediated scenarios via SAP Integration Suite, or side-by-side extensibility on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). It also covers the authentication mechanisms available in SCV2 and how they compare and, in many ways, contrast with what SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C) offered.

Having worked in the SAP CX space for over 12 years across SAP C4C, SAP Field Service Management (FSM), SAP Customer Data Cloud (Gigya), and SAP Marketing Cloud, our recent implementation on SAP Sales/Service Cloud V2 gained some significant learnings, particularly around how the integration and extensibility model has fundamentally evolved from the C4C era.

The following abbreviations are used throughout this blog:

SAP C4C - SAP Cloud for Customer

SAP SSCV2 - SAP Sales/Service Cloud V2 (latest version of the product)

 

What is a Communication System?

In both SAP C4C and Sales/Service Cloud V2, a Communication System represents an external logical system instance, such as a SAP BTP subaccount, an ERP back-end, or a middleware platform that exchanges data with Sales Cloud. Think of it as the identity card you issue to a partner system before it can walk in the door.

 

SAP C4C Baseline — What We're Moving Away From

SAP Cloud for Customer's integration model has the authentication methods that support the administrators to have two choices:

  • Basic Authentication

  • SSL / X.509 Certificates

Why this matters now: Basic Authentication is being phased out across SAP's landscape. SAP SuccessFactors and SAP BTP have already announced sunset timelines for Basic Auth on their APIs. Organisations must migrate their integrations to OAuth 2.0 before this deadline to prevent disruptions.

 

SAP Sales/Service Cloud V2 Communication System — A Richer Model

SAP Sales/Service Cloud V2 provides a future-proof solution with multiple options to the SAP partners/customers.

When you create a Communication System in Sales Cloud V2 (via Settings → Integration → Communication Systems), you'll immediately notice it's structured around two distinct directions, each with its own authentication model.

 

 

Deleted Communication Systems

A key improvement in SAP SSCV2 is the ability to restore the Communication System after it is deleted.

An Administrator can delete a Communication System, and it is moved to the Deleted Communication Systems rather than being removed from the system entirely, unlike in SAP C4C.

Steps to Delete a Communication System

  1. Change the Status of the Communication System from Active to Inactive

  1. Click the Delete button

  2. Deleted Communication System visible in the section below:

  3. Remaining Days shows the number of days until the System removes it from the Deletion table in SAP SSCV2. Clicking the Undo button will make the Communication System Inactive

  4. Clicking the Delete button will permanently delete it from SAP SSCV2.

The retention time for the Deleted Communication System is 60 days. After 60 days, it is permanently deleted and cannot be recovered.

 

Authentication Methods: Comparison between SAP C4C and SAP SSCV2

The table below lists the authentication methods available for Inbound and Outbound connectivity with SAP SSCV2.

The ideal scenario would be to integrate SAP SSCV2 with SAP Integration Suite and use SAP BTP (Kyma or Cloud Foundry) for extensibility.

 

DirectionTypeSAP C4CSAP SSCV2Notable Changes

Inbound

Basic Authentication (Username / Password)

Business user controlled by Business Roles assigned & Technical User authentication.

Technical User authentication is controlled by the Technical Roles assigned. Clear separation between a Technical User and a business user

Improved access control on Technical User and Technical Roles in SAP SSCV2

Inbound

SSL Client Certificates

Key pair certificate created in C4C, middleware, or an external system.

Key pair certificate created in a middleware or an external system.

Remains the same with a different user experience in SAP SSCV2

Outbound

Basic Authentication (Username / Password)

A username and password provided by a middleware or an external system that has access to the respective API.

A username and password provided by a middleware or an external system that has access to the respective API.

Remains the same with a different user experience in SAP SSCV2

Outbound

SSL Certificate Authentication

SAP trusted, or a 3rd party trusted Key pair certificate downloadable from Outbound Communication from C4C

Keypair certificate created in BTP, middleware with a passphrase uploaded in SSCV2.

A key pair certificate can only be created in external systems and uploaded to SAP SSCV2

Outbound

OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials Grant

NA

The Client fetches the access token and passes the token in the subsequent step to get access to the resources

New feature in SAP SSCV2

Outbound

OAuth 2.0 SAML Bearer Assertion

NA

Principal propagation is achieved through the OAuth2 SAML Bearer assertion flow. The sender app uses a custom identity provider that also acts as a SAML token issuer.

New feature in SAP SSCV2

Outbound

OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange

NA

Get a new token for an existing token with a different scope and audience, and pass the new token to access the resources.

New feature in SAP SSCV2



Recommendation: Don't use the Basic authentication method in productive tenants for security reasons

How to choose the right Authentication method

Choosing the right authentication method in SAP SSCV2 is not purely a technical decision; it also has implications for security governance, audit compliance, and the operational overhead your basis and integration teams will carry throughout the life of the implementation. The right choice depends on three major things:

  • Who or what is calling SAP SSCV2

  • Whether a human user's identity needs to be traceable in the system

  • Whether middleware sits between the caller and SAP SSCV2.

The table below summarises the recommendations based on our experience by scenario:

 

Scenario

Recommended Authentication Method

Pros

Cons

Direct API Call using Basic Auth, where no possibility of maintaining the Certificate or OAuth

Basic Auth (Technical User/Technical Role)

  • Familiar, quick to set up, and works without any additional infrastructure.

  • Appropriate for development sandboxes and early-phase integration testing

  • SAP SSCV2 provides better control through features such as Technical User and Technical Roles, which provide granular access to APIs.

  • Credentials are long-lived, shared between systems, and must be rotated manually, all of which introduces risk as the integration landscape grows.

  • Basic Authentication, in most cases, should be treated as a starting point, not a target state.

For integrations running through SAP Cloud Integration

Client Certificate Authentication

  • The Communication System in SCV2 is configured with the Integration Suite tenant's certificate on the inbound side.

  • SAP SSCV2 uses the BTP-generated certificate from the service instance for outbound calls into Integration Suite iFlows

  • Similar to integration patterns with SAP C4C

  • The certificate validity period is visible and auditable.

  • Initial Setup is required, and technical knowledge of certificate work is required

 

For SAP BTP side-by-side extensions

OAuth 2.0 with SAML Bearer Assertion

  • Passes the context of the logged-in user from and to SAP SSCV2. This is a pattern that enabled Principal Propagation

  • This is a new feature that was not available with C4C

  • An application embedded in SAP SSCV2 as a Mashup that runs in SAP BTP (created from SAP Build or apps running in Cloud Foundry or Kyma) will capture the actual logged-in user in the change logs in SAP SSCV2 rather than a generic technical user.

  • Since the logged-in user context is being passed, the logged-in user Business Role must have sufficient access to the API calls made in the application. Business Role must be managed in such a way

  • SAP Cloud Identity Service is required as a prerequisite.

  • The user should be available in the BTP subaccount has the necessary roles in BTP if the application is calling an integration flow.

 

Server-Server Calls (Ex: Custom Entity Service, Direct Non-SAP integrations)

OAuth 2.0 – Client Credentials

  • This is used to access an external service directly from the backend of Sales/Service Cloud V2 by using a dedicated Client ID.

  • Scopes can be configured to restrict what that token can access, which is especially valuable when integrating with systems outside the SAP landscape.

  • User context is not passed to the external service

 

In practice, the final choice of authentication method will be shaped by factors specific to each client's landscape and internal security policies.

Key considerations include:

  • Custom Identity Provider (IdP) availability

  • Enterprise security standards

  • Certificate lifecycle management

  • Existing integration platform capabilities

 

In my next blog, I will walk through the step-by-step configuration of the recommended authentication methods in SAP Sales/Service Cloud V2, covering Basic Auth using a Technical User, Client Certificate setup for SAP Integration Suite and OAuth 2.0 SAML Bearer Assertion for SAP BTP side-by-side extensions.

Thanks, 

Adi

entry.title: What We Learned Hosting a SAP Document AI Workshop
entry.content:
Introduction

Some weeks ago we had a hands-on event focused on SAP Document AI and enterprise document automation. We had planned it to be just a technical workshop, yet the attendees wasted no time in telling us all kinds of complications they were experiencing at the time. Soon, we were talking about operational processes, workflow complexity, and the increasing importance of AI in enterprise environments.

When we were planning the workshop we did not just want to show people a product. Our goal was to create a space where participants could engage directly with actual document processing situations, as well as investigate automation options that might enhance their routine workflows. I mean, you can't have an opinion about something you haven't used or experienced yourself.

During the session attendees had the opportunity to experiment with document classification, extraction models, workflow routing and automation processes related to larger SAP workflows.

As the Hackathon went on, the conversations naturally shifted toward some of the operational challenges many companies are currently facing. Although SAP Document AI was the focus, there was also room for repetitive manual work, fragmented validation processes, inconsistent document formats, and the difficulty of scaling document-heavy operations across teams and business units.

 

What the workshop was about

The workshop included product exploration and practical activities, simulating real enterprise scenarios. Rather than merely attend presentations or demos, participants were able to directly engage with workflows, classifiers, extraction schemas, and automation processes.

The event provided participants with insights into how SAP Document AI can support invoicing processes, free-form documents, workflow routing, and document classification.

 

One exercise that participants seemed to find particularly useful was the ROI calculator we brought to the workshop. While most of the day focused on workflows and automation scenarios, the calculator helped ground those discussions in something every organization cares about: business impact. It gave attendees a way to estimate how document automation could affect their own operations and helped move the conversation from technical possibilities to practical outcomes.

I think we were really taken aback by the diversity of perspectives in the room. People came from different industries and business environments, and brought very different operational realities and use cases to the discussion.

We were surprised that many of the issues being discussed were incredibly similar. Within all industries, companies had a lot of the same challenges around managing documents, approvals, integrations and keeping processes consistent. 

While working on practical document-processing scenarios, it became evident that the challenges most companies face go far beyond document extraction. As operations expand across multiple departments and teams, many organizations are still faced with fragmented workflows, repetitive manual validations, inconsistent document formats and processes that are becoming harder to manage.

And the workshop also reinforced something we often see when working with SAP technologies: real conversations happen once people can interact directly with a solution. The hackathon became much more practical and grounded after participants started to set up schemas, test classifiers and experiment with workflows. Rather than considering AI as something far away or theoretical, attendees began to consider how these solutions could realistically be integrated into their own operational environments.

Another important conclusion we arrived at was the importance of integration for successful AI adoption. Precise extraction models are crucial, no one will deny that, but they are just one part of the process. Companies also require workflows capable of adapting to operational changes, handling variations between document types, and collaborating effectively over time with existing systems.

Some Reflections

One of the most gratifying aspects of the workshop was to look back and see how quickly participants started to relate the exercises to their own organizations. Throughout the day the conversation evolved easily from examples under discussion to real business processes, internal priorities and potential next steps. Watching that transition in real time was a reminder of how the value of these sessions often goes far beyond the tech itself.

The workshop was also a great opportunity for us to hear directly from organizations that are facing different operational challenges, and to get a better understanding of how they are approaching document automation and AI adoption within their SAP landscapes.

Some of the most valuable insights came not only from the practical tasks but from the conversations that took place around them. The people who came had different perspectives, experiences and business contexts and we learnt from each other. The workshop was a valuable experience for all involved. As for me, I’m already looking forward to the next one, whether that’s as a host, a participant, or simply someone eager to keep learning.

entry.title: What's New in the SAP Green Ledger Analytical Dashboard in Q2 2026
entry.content:
New Here?

The SAP Green Ledger Analytical Dashboard is an SAP Analytics Cloud story that puts greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and financial data on the same canvas: KPIs, trend charts, a CO2e flow statement, and a financial statement with CO2e quantities. It's built on top of SAP Datasphere (data layer) and SAP Analytics Cloud (presentation layer), and ships as ready-to-import business content.

For the full tour of pages, KPIs, filters, and how the calculations work, see Analyze Carbon Data Alongside Financial Data | SAP Help Portal in the SAP Green Ledger documentation. The setup walkthrough lives in the Install SAP Green Ledger Analytical Dashboard | SAP Help Portal.

 

What's new in Q2/2026?

Release time: May 18, 2026. SAC content version 1.5.1, Datasphere content version 1.5.1.

 

A cleaner look with the Horizon theme

The dashboard now uses the Horizon theme, aligning it visually with the rest of the SAP Fiori experience. 

 

Units spelled out, not abbreviated

  • KG → Kilogram
  • TO → Ton

 

New WBS Element filter

You can now slice carbon and financial data by Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) element, so project-level analysis is finally possible directly in the dashboard.

 

Sharper KPI logic for Costs of Goods Sold

The semantic tag used to calculate Costs of Goods Sold changed from RECO_COGS to COGS. This makes the calculation simpler and aligns it with standard financial tagging.

 

CO2e Flow Statement: all filters available

All filters now apply to the CO2e Flow Statement on page 3, so the same filtering you use elsewhere in the dashboard works here too.

 

Cleaner page-level filters

  • End Period and Comparison End Period moved to page 4 as page-level filters.

  • Fiscal Year Period now defaults to the current fiscal year, so the dashboard is useful the moment you open it. 

 

German translation now available

The dashboard is now available in German. You can edit your preferred language in Settings from the shell bar. To enable it, change the display language. For more information, see Edit Your User Settings.

 

New how-to guide: updating content

For customers already running an earlier version of the business content, the new Update Existing Business Content | SAP Help Portal topic provides a structured path to the latest release. 

 

Troubleshooting

For known issues and resolutions, see Troubleshooting. If the issue persists, contact technical support.

 

What's next

We'd love to hear from you:

  • Which dashboard pages do you use most?
  • Are there filters or KPIs you wish were there?
  • Did the documentation save you a support ticket, or expose a step we should clarify?

Leave your comment below and let us know what's working for you, what you'd like to see next, and how we can keep making the SAP Green Ledger experience better!

entry.title: SLT Rule Assignment – “Field LAST_ACTION_CD does not exist in table despite field being added
entry.content:
Hello experts,

I am currently working on SLT trigger-based replication (ECC → target) and trying to implement a Rule Assignment with a custom include to derive a field LAST_ACTION_CD. My requirement is to change the operation flag so  I can distinguish between archived records and physically deleted records since both records have operation flag  = "D".

However, I am facing an issue where SLT throws the following errors during source setup:

"Source setup failed due to the following error: Cannot save filter settings for object VBAK Cannot save filter settings for object VBAK Field LAST_ACTION_CD does not exist in table VBAK"

What I have done

1. Table Enhancement in SLT

In LTRS → Table Settings → VBAK → Table Structure:

  • I added a new field:
    Field Name: LAST_ACTION_CD
Data Type: CHAR
Length: 1
  • The field is visible in: Modified Table Structure as shown in the image

2. Rule Assignment

In Rule Assignment, I created:

  • Rule Type: Field-Related

  • Assignment Target: LAST_ACTION_CD

  • Implementation: Custom include program

  • Import Parameters:

    I_P1 = HARD_DELETE_BUSS
I_P2 = HARD_DELETE_ARCH

3. Custom Include

I implemented a custom include (Z_*_LAST_ACTION_CD_A)  using SE80 to populate LAST_ACTION_CD based on IUUC_OPERAT_FLAG.

Despite all these and regeneration with program IUUC_REDEFINE_DB_TRIGGERS , I am still getting error in datasphere replication flow that the field does not exist. 

How do I fix it and does anyone has experience in changing the operation flag. 

We are on SAP ECC and DMIS 2018

Thanks,

Bhat

entry.title: Re: Managing Pre-approvals using Attendance Quota in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
@PetraRana 

Please find below the answers to your questions:

1. Can EXTRA time type be configured to deduct Attendance Quota?

Ans: Yes EXTRA time type can be configure in Attendance Quota. I have added a screenshot where I used the EXTRA time type to add this to a time type group

It works for all time valuations (Route input based on Decision, Filter Input Groups and Aggregate Input Groups and Split) where Attendance Quota variables are used.

2. Can the Quota Limit be calculated by system automatically? For example we have use case when we have annual overtime limit which has to be prorated for new starters. Or can the attendance quota only be created by Employees on need basis based on the template configured by the Administrator?

Ans: Quota limit cannot be calculated by the system automatically. Attendance Quota currently can only be created for employees for your use case. Prorated overtime limit will required to be calculated and assigned to the Quota Limit field for the employee. It can also be configured by Administrators without the need of a workflow.

3. Can Attendance Quotas be generated in mass for employees without the employee having to request it? Our use case is to allocate the overtime limit at the beginning of calendar year or on employee hire automatically without the need for each employee requesting the attendance quota at the beginning of the year.

Ans: Attendance Quota can be generated in mass without employee having to request by uploading via Import and Export Data. You could as well use the Global Variable Value to set the default value and override when the employee has a changed value. This would also work without having to create for all the employees.

entry.title: External Vendor PO GR via MIGO for EWM Plants (WO IBD)
entry.content:
Many occasions we need flexible way to receive the external vendor POs into the warehouse (EWM) without creating inbound delivery due to lack ASN confirmation from vendors or other operational issues. 

Recently i learned about Synchronous Goods Movement Process which allows external vendor POs can be received vi MIGO without an inbound delivery to be created 

Pre-Requisites:

1. Synchronous Goods Movement functionality is activated

2. POs are created without confirmation control key

3. Pack Specs are created with correct condition type (this step is only required if you want to build the HU for label printing etc).

Configuration:

1. SIMG –> SCM Extended Warehouse Management–> Extended Warehouse Management –> Cross-Process Settings -> Handling Units -> Package Building –> Define Package Building Profile.

2. SIMG –> SCM Extended Warehouse Management –> Extended Warehouse Management –> Interfaces –> ERP Integration –> Goods Movements –> Activate Synchronous Goods Movement Posting.

3. SIMG –> SCM Extended Warehouse Management –> Extended Warehouse Management –> Interfaces –> ERP Integration –> Goods Movements –> Make Settings for Synchronous Goods Movement Posting

4.  SIMG –> SCM Extended Warehouse Management –> Extended Warehouse Management –> Cross-Process Settings –> Warehouse Task -> Define Warehouse Process Type

Note : make sure, Auto WT creation at GR is active

5.  SIMG –> SCM Extended Warehouse Management –> Extended Warehouse Management –> Cross-Process Settings –> Warehouse Task -> Determine Warehouse Process Type.

Note : there are two process indicators available for Vendor PO GR related (Packed & Unpacked)

Additionally, for the respective Storage type, we need to maintain the storage type search sequence and Storage bin assignment.

Testing Process: 

1. PO Created without confirmation control key

2. MIGO 

3. WT display at MIGO

 

for reference purpose, i am sharing the pack spec which i have created for the above material 

Conclusion:

With this process, we have observed that, external vendor POs can be received into EWM locations without an inbound delivery, and it simplifies goods receiving process and also with reference to palletization data we can build the HUs and print the labels.

 

Thank you to all my mentors!
Hari Sivvala

https://learning.sap.com/courses/basic-customizing-in-sap-s-4hana-ewm/posting-goods-receipt-for-embedded-ewm

entry.title: The business requires a discount field at the Purchase Order item
entry.content:
The business requires a discount field at the Purchase Order item line level in the Create Purchase Order app. Currently, SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud allows discounts to be maintained only through the Conditions tab using condition types. There is no standard discount column available in the PO item overview screen.

We would like to understand whether SAP provides any standard functionality, configuration, personalization, or extensibility option to enable a line-level discount field where users can directly enter a discount amount or percentage without navigating to the Conditions tab.

This requirement is intended to simplify PO creation and improve user efficiency during purchasing transactions.

entry.title: Changing the unit of measure during RF packing in SAP EWM
entry.content:
Introduction

In material and engineering manufacturing industries, products are often managed and sold in more than one unit of measure. A typical example is steel bars: stocked and sold by length (1 m, 2 m, 3 m, etc.), yet some customers require delivery in KG, or warehouse teams need to repack stock into KG before outbound shipment.

SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) supports this scenario as a standard operation through the RF/mobile device packing transaction - no custom development required. This blog walks through the end-to-end configuration and testing steps.

Business scenario

  • Product: Steel Bar
  • Base / ordering UoM: M (metres)
  • Alternative UoM: KG

Requirement: During outbound packing on the RF device, the operator repacks a quantity of steel bars into a destination HU expressed in KG

Define Unit of Measurement Attributes (prerequisite)

This is a critical step that is easy to overlook. For the UoM switch to work during RF packing, the alternative unit must be flagged as Stock-Specific UoM at the warehouse level.

IMG path: EWM > Master Data > Product > Define Unit of Measurement Attributes

Define Unit of Measurement Attributes - KG flagged as Stock-Specific UoM for warehouse

Create Exception code

IMG path: EWM > Cross-Process settings > Exception Handling > Create Exception Codes

  • Warehouse:XXXX
  • Exception Code: AUOM (or any 4-character key, e.g. ZUOM, XAUO)
  • Description: Change AUoM

     

    Assign the business context

    Navigate to child menu Define Exception Code > Details and set the following:

    • Business Context: PIM – Packing/Unpacking Products (Internal Movement)
    • Execution Step: A0 (background)
    • Exception Code Profile: assign only if restricting to a specific user group


Exception Code details - Business Context: PIM Packing/Unpacking, Execution Step: A0

Within the exception code configuration, maintain the process parameter for differences. Set Difference Category to blank - this ensures no stock difference is posted to a bin, account, or delivery when the UoM is switched. The system converts the quantity using the material master conversion factor.

Assign Internal Exception Code to your Exception Code

Navigate to:  IMG > EWM > Cross-process setting > Exception Handling > Assign Internal Exception Codes to Exception Codes

Create a new entry mapping your external exception code to SAP's delivered internal exception code AUOM - "Change Quantity and/or Unit". This is the link that allows EWM to trigger the UoM change function automatically when the exception is raised during RF packing. Assign Internal Exception Codes - external code mapped to internal AUOM (Change Quantity and/or Unit)

Testing - RF device

Navigate to: RFUI > Outbound Process > Packing > Manually Repack HU Item

Scan the source HU RF device - scan the source HU containing the steel bars

Select item and switch UoM

The system displays the item (Steel Bar for Export, 3 M). Press SF2 (UoMC) - the UoM field opens and becomes editable. Change to KG and enter the required quantity (e.g. 5 KG). RF device - press SF2 (UoMC) to open the UoM field; item currently shows 3 M


Assign destination HU

Enter the destination HU (pre-created, or create in real time using F4 HUCrt). The screen now confirms AQty = 5 KG with the destination HU and storage bin. 

 RF device - destination HU assigned, quantity confirmed as 5 KG

Result - HU overview

After confirming, the HU overview shows the stock correctly split across both handling units:HU overview - source HU retains remaining metres; destination HU holds 5 KG


Desktop packing transaction

The same UoM conversion is available in the /SCWM/PACK desktop work center (Work Center Pack in Goods Issue) without any additional exception code configuration. The Alt. Unit and Quantity columns are available directly in the packing item list.

 /SCWM/PACK desktop transaction - UoM conversion available with no extra configuration

 

Summary

This standard SAP EWM capability is particularly relevant for industries handling dual-unit materials: steel, aluminium, wire, timber, fabric, bulk chemicals, and similar products.

  • Enable Stock-Specific UoM for the alternative unit in warehouse settings - this is the key prerequisite
  • Configure an exception code mapped to SAP's internal AUOM exception
  • Warehouse operators can switch UoM directly on the RF device during packing
  • No stock discrepancies are posted - the system converts using the material master factor

The same operation is also available in the /SCWM/PACK desktop transaction with no extra config

I hope this guide is helpful. Open for feedback and suggestions - please share your experience or questions in the comments below.

entry.title: SAP Joule for Developers is now available for lower releases of SAP Cloud ERP Private.
entry.content:
SAP Joule for Developers as a Side-by-Side ABAP AI Service Offering for Lower Releases of SAP Cloud ERP Private (SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition)

Joule speaks ABAP, and now it speaks to more systems than ever before.

SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities have already created strong interest across the SAP ecosystem. From customers to partners, one message has been consistent, make these powerful developer capabilities available for lower SAP S/4HANA releases as well.

The feedback was clear, and SAP has delivered.

Joule for Developers is now being extended to SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud 2021 and higher, covering systems on SAP_BASIS 756 and above. This means organizations running earlier private cloud releases can now start benefiting from AI-assisted ABAP development sooner, without waiting for their next major upgrade cycle.

Why side by side architecture?

The introduction of the new ABAP AI side-by-side architecture marks a strategic step toward making AI capabilities more accessible, scalable, and future-ready across diverse SAP landscapes. By decoupling AI services from release dependencies, SAP can more rapidly deliver new capabilities powered by both SAP-provided ABAP AI models and leading third-party LLM providers.

One of the key advantages is simplified adoption. Customers can now enable ABAP AI capabilities through a more streamlined onboarding process using SAP for Me and Central Business Configuration, reducing setup effort while ensuring consistent lifecycle management across environments.

Another major benefit is release-independent access. Instead of being tightly coupled to specific SAP S/4HANA release cycles, customers can consume ABAP AI services side by side with their existing systems. This enables even lower-release Private Edition customers to leverage the latest AI innovations without waiting for a system upgrade.

In short, the side-by-side model is not just an architecture shift, it is an innovation enabler, helping customers adopt ABAP AI faster, more flexibly, and with greater future readiness.

Commercial model & AI Units

SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities are embedded in the Joule Premium package and offered with separate licenses for customers and partners. Pricing follows a consumption-based model, meaning you only pay for actual usage. Customers and partners purchase AI Units and activate the required packages via SAP for Me. Authorization for developer access is managed through Cloud Identity Services, while AI requests are executed and metered in the ABAP AI Hub 2.0 on SAP BTP. 

What is an SAP AI Unit?

SAP AI Units are a form of virtual currency that customers purchase to activate and use premium AI capabilities within SAP Cloud Services. They are consumption-based credits that can be used flexibly across multiple SAP solutions and are not tied to a specific product or line of business. This allows customers to draw from a central pool of AI Units as they use AI-powered features across their SAP landscape. AI Units are purchased annually and expire after 12 months if not used.Customers can track their usage and remaining balance in real time via SAP for Me.

How do I know how many units I should purchase?

SAP provides guidance and estimation tools to help determine the right amount of AI Units. Your SAP Account Executive or Partner can support you in defining the appropriate quantity and help model different usage scenarios. 

How to Purchase the License and activate the packages? 

A.    For Customers

Purchase AI Units using SKU 8019164 via SAP for Me to begin the Premium AI activation process.

B.    For partners

Place the order for cloud test, demo, and development for SAP AI Unit (8019670).  

Configuring the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities for Side-by-Side Use (Administrators)

Prerequisites

You are an administrator with full access to SAP for Me.

Procedure

  1. Open SAP for Me and navigate to Portfolio & Products.
     
  2. Select the tab My Product Packages and choose Technology Platform AI Agents and Features.
  3. Select the tab Applications and choose the application SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities.
  4. Now you can Start Provisioning.
    1. Enter the Solution Details.
    2. Select a Cloud Identity Services system.
    3. Select your region.
    4. Choose a Formation Name (the name is mandatory but has no further technical relevance).
    5. Review and finish the setup. The provision process might take few moments to complete. The new solution will achieve the Ready status when all tenants are successfully provisioned and integrations are fully established. Go to the resources tab to view the status of the generated resources.
  5. Activate the feature in SAP Central Business Configuration as described here.
  6. Assign named users through SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) to enable access for authorized users.
  7. In the tab Resources you will find a new tenant providing its Main URL (which will lead to the UI of the application).

     



  8. Provide that URL to the developers so they can configure SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities for side-by-side use in ABAP development tools for Eclipse.

Configuring the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities in ABAP System for Side-by-Side Use (For Developers)

Prerequisites

Your SAP administrator provides you a URL that refers to the SAP BTP.

Configure the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities for Specific ABAP Projects

To use the latest SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities in specific ABAP projects in your ADT client.

  1. In the Project Explorer, select the relevant ABAP project and Choose Properties from the context menu
  2. Select ABAP Development Joule .To make the input available, select the Enable project-specific settings checkbox.
  3. Beneath the Use Joule side-by-side radio button, enter the URL to the subscription of the central BTP service on the SAP BTP.
    Optional: Choose the Check Connection button to control if the connection works.


  4. Confirm with Apply.
  5. You can now use the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities side-by-side in the selected ABAP project.

Availability of SAP Joule for Developers Capabilities Across SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition Releases

ABAP AI Capability

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2023

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2022

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2021

Predictive Code Completion

Supported

Supported

Supported

ABAP/CDS code explanation

Supported

Supported

Supported

ABAP Unit Test Generation

Supported

Supported

Supported

CDS Test Generation

Supported

Supported

Supported

OData UI Service from Scratch*

No

No

No

RAP Business Logic Prediction

No

No

No

More details can be found here:

  1. Sapphire 2026: GA announcement for agentic ABAP AI (Sonja Lienard)
  2. Sapphire 2026: ABAP AI - Chapter 3: We go agentic! (Alexander Rother)
  3. How to purchase Agentic ABAP AI
  4. VS Code: ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code is now available on the VS Code marketplace! 
  5. FAQ document for frequent questions
  6. Public Roadmap: SAP Help / SAP Roadmap Explorer
  7. Help & Guides
    1. Free Promo only (not yet AI Units): Step-by-step guide how to activate free promo for SAP Joule for ...
    2. Free Promo only (not yet AI Units): Joule for Developers (J4D) for S/4HANA Public Cloud Setup Guide
    3. SAP ABAP AI Discovery Center
    4. SAP BTP Discovery Center (Free Tier Services)
    5. Custom code migration to SAP S/4HANA powered by SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities
    6. YouTube playlist for Joule for Developers (J4D)
    7. Joule for Developers Help (J4D)
    8. Build SAP Fiori Apps with ABAP Cloud and SAP Joule for developers (RAP120) 
    9. ADT Eclipse AI Capabilities Help
    10. ABAP AI SDK Help
    11. ABAP-1 Help

More high-level insights about Joule for Developers/ABAP AI and ABAP platform can be found here .

 


Co-Authors - @karl_kessler @niloofar_naseri

entry.title: Re: Functional Currency Transactions
entry.content:
Hi Sanne,

To answer your question directly: Yes, this is a limitation of this specific API. The standard "G/L Account Line Items - Read (A2X)" (API_GLACCOUNTLINEITEM_SRV) does not expose an explicit field named AmountInFunctionalCurrency.

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the Functional Currency is not a separate predefined technical field within these standard OData APIs. Instead, based on your CBC configuration, the Functional Currency is designated to correspond to an existing currency type. This is often the Group Currency, or optionally, a Freely Defined Currency type (if you have configured and chosen to use one).

Because of this, the balances for the functional currency are already in the API payload, but they are extracted through the field of whichever currency type it was mapped to (e.g., AmountInGroupCurrency or AmountInFreeDefinedCurrency1 fields).

To resolve this for your customer, I would suggest two paths:

1. Check existing fields (Quickest way): Could you verify in the customer's configuration which exact Currency Type the Functional Currency is mapped to? If you check the current API payload for that corresponding field, you should find the functional currency balances there, even though it's named differently.

2. Build a Custom API (If explicit naming is mandatory): If the customer's external integration strictly requires a field explicitly labeled as AmountInFunctionalCurrency, the standard best practice is to use In-App Extensibility. Standard CDS Views (such as I_GLAccountLineItem or I_OperationalAcctgDocItem) do natively contain the AmountInFunctionalCurrency measure. You can build a Custom CDS View based on one of these standard views using scenario External API and select the exact fields you need.

Hope this clarifies the API limitation and helps you move forward!

Best regards,

Michal Palečko

entry.title: Re: SLT Rule Assignment – “Field LAST_ACTION_CD does not exist in table despite field being added
entry.content: Interesting use case. A few things I would verify: 1. Check whether the field was added only in the SLT table structure definition or whether the runtime object metadata was also regenerated successfully. 2. Verify that the field LAST_ACTION_CD is visible in the generated logging table and corresponding runtime structures after running IUUC_REDEFINE_DB_TRIGGERS. 3. If the table is already loaded and active in a configuration, try removing and re-adding the table settings/rule assignment and regenerate the objects again. 4. Ensure the field name is referenced exactly as defined in the modified table structure (case and spelling). 5. Since the error occurs during source setup, it may indicate that the rule framework validation cannot find the field in the generated runtime metadata rather than in the custom include itself. Also, regarding distinguishing archived versus physically deleted records, I would be interested to know whether anyone has successfully leveraged IUUC_OPERAT_FLAG together with custom rule assignments on DMIS 2018, as there may be release-specific limitations. Please share the resolution once identified—it is a valuable scenario for SLT-to-Datasphere replication projects.

entry.title: Re: Clarification on Datasphere Live Connection Scope in SAC
entry.content:
Dear China Nageswararao Kalava

The connection to Datasphere is to the 'whole' tenant, if you like. What the user has access to within that Datasphere tenant is determined by the roles (and scoped roles) assigned to that user. If the user is assigned a single-scoped role with a scope of 1 space, that user will only have access to that space. 

For more about this topic, my blog, video and article are a good starting point

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-datasphere-security-amp-data-access-controls-overview/ba-p/13805353

and 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-datasphere-space-and-lifecycle-management/ba-p/13603496

 

kind regards, Matthew

entry.title: SAP Engagement Cloud Q2 2026 Release
entry.content:
This year is shaping up to be one of transformation. In our Q1 release, we unveiled SAP Engagement Cloud as the evolution of our omnichannel marketing solution into an orchestrated, real-time engagement solution for organizations that need to connect data, processes, and decisions across the business. 

That evolution is even more critical given the shift in how work gets done, with AI and agents increasingly executing tasks at the direction of marketers. Now, as part of the Autonomous CX suite of solutions, SAP Engagement Cloud continues to grow with our Q2 release, strengthening data foundations, unlocking new engagement channels, and delivering more powerful AI capabilities to accelerate execution and improve customer experiences. 

These advancements come to life throughout our Q2 release, including through our strategic partnership with Google. From a shared data foundation to rich messaging experiences to AI-assisted content creation, the fruits of this collaborative partnership are showing up for marketers in tangible ways across this release. Read on for the latest innovations. 

AI & Analytics 

AI is becoming a core part of how marketers plan, create, and optimize campaigns, and this release takes that AI-first focus even further, embedding AI at the point of execution and establishing the foundation for fully agentic workflows. 

Joule with SAP Engagement Cloud (Early Adopter Care) 

Joule is coming to SAP Engagement Cloud Enterprise Edition as an Early Adopter Care solution, bringing natural language interaction to campaign management. To start, you can duplicate campaigns instantly or find exactly what you need by using simple prompts. For example, you can ask Joule to "List campaigns running during Black Friday promotion 2025,” then “Create a copy” of a campaign you wish to repurpose for 2026, without searching or starting from scratch. This means you can spend less time on manual tasks and more time on strategic and creative work that moves the business forward.  

AI-assisted Content Composer (Pilot) 

Easily create images and text in seconds, directly within a block-based email using this generative AI feature. Powered by Google’s Gemini models, it can help you:  

  • Fix image inconsistencies in the moment, such as product alignment, rather than waiting for source updates in the product catalog 
  • Enhance existing brand and product visuals, such as adding or removing backgrounds 
  • Generate new images or copy that fit the campaign goals and will resonate with the audience's motivations 
  • Create scenes that feature multiple products in a setting or on a model 

Importantly, generated content is grounded in the context of the audience segment, products, and related campaigns, to ensure that text and images are always relevant, consistent, and compliant with brand standards. And everything is editable and previewable right inside the email editor, so there’s no need to jump between image editing tools, like Photoshop, or start from scratch. 

AI-assisted Report Builder: Mobile App Channel Support (Generally Available) 

The AI-assisted Report Builder now includes Mobile In-App and Mobile Push channel data sets. This lets you get instant insights and trends on your campaign performance across these channels without switching tools or rebuilding reports. Simply ask your question, get your answer, and Report Builder handles the rest. For example, ask for “key metrics from Mobile In-App campaigns sent in the last month,” and AI handles the analysis, surfacing results instantly. 

Recommendation Center (Pilot) 

Deliver smarter product recommendations with a scalable, AI-driven experience powered by a new, flexible personalization engine. In pilot now for Email, create, test, and adapt personalization strategies in one place, using real-time data (e.g., product availability) and customer signals (e.g., category interest) to ensure every recommendation is relevant and timely. Adjust underlying models (logic) to continuously optimize performance and avoid redundant suggestions. Then fine-tune results with advanced filtering and customizable business rules to deliver more relevant, impactful experiences across every channel, with less effort.  

Event Attribution in Open Data (Generally Available) 

Event Attribution data is now available in Open Data, making it straightforward to connect attribution insights to your existing analytics stack for deeper and more flexible reporting. Gain a better understanding of what is driving performance, which makes it easier to measure impact and optimize your campaigns.  

“Developing our engagement strategy has been a continuous process. We didn't start everything at once. We worked closely with SAP Engagement Cloud to build everything up over time, and today we’ve reached a place where our personalization and campaigns have become much more sophisticated. And using the insights into our data and engagement, we’re still continuously optimizing.” 
Jens Krahl. Head of CRM & Marketing Technology, lastminute.com 

Content & Channels 

As marketers, you know it’s important to engage customers on their terms. This release expands how—and where—you connect with your audience, making engagement more seamless and effective. 

User-initiated Message Handling (Early Adopter Care) 

User-initiated Message Handling is coming to all supported Conversational Channels as an Early Adopter Care feature. This empowers you to respond to user-initiated keyword triggers, such as "order" for shipping updates or "help" to hand off to a human agent. This solution also allows WhatsApp users to unsubscribe from messages initiated by the brand, which are outside the active conversation window (24 hours). The end result is a win for everyone: faster answers to customers’ questions, more control for your contacts, and cleaner engagement data for you. 

LINE in Audience-based Automation (Generally Available) 

LINE now supports Audience-based Automation programs. Use a segment or contact list to reach your LINE audience at scale—the same way you already do for other channels. For instance, you can easily notify subscribed customers about their upcoming replenishment order. 

SAP Engagement Cloud SDK (Early Adopter Care) 

We’re moving the SAP Engagement Cloud SDK to Early Adopter Care. This is an industry-first toolkit that unifies integration for Android, iOS, and Web into a single, natively multiplatform solution to help you:  

  • Streamline implementation 
  • Improve performance 
  • Bring uniform, cookieless first-party data collection across all mobile and web touchpoints. 

This solution will initially support Mobile Push, Web Push, and In-App Messaging channels, but also serves as the foundation for future mobile and web-based channels, such as Embedded Messaging. 

Apps & Channel Configuration (Pilot) 

A new unified Apps & Channel Configuration page—exclusive to the SAP Engagement Cloud SDK—is in pilot. Consolidating three currently separate interfaces into one, the new interface has two tabs: the Applications tab, to register mobile and web applications, each generating an app code used for SDK integration; and the Channel Configurations tab, where clients create reusable configurations—such as push notification credentials —that can be assigned to one or more applications. When a channel configuration is updated, those changes are automatically reflected across all assigned applications, eliminating the need to manage settings in multiple places. 

Embedded Messaging (Pilot) 

This persistent, pull-based messaging channel delivers inbox experiences inside mobile apps and websites—with no push opt-in required. For example, if your customer receives a loyalty reward but isn’t ready to act immediately, the message persists in their in-app or on-site inbox until they are—no more digging through email or spam filters required. Unlike push notifications, Embedded Messaging messages stay in the inbox until the contact is ready to engage, making it a reliable, brand-owned channel that fits naturally into an omnichannel strategy. 

Setup and operation are also straightforward: once the SAP Engagement Cloud SDK is integrated and the app is configured on the Apps & Channel Configuration page, marketers can operate the channel entirely independently, reducing technical overhead. Compose messages using Content Blocks and apply Personalization tokens, product recommendations, and automation, all supported out of the box. 

Embedded Messaging in Campaign Analytics (Pilot) 

As part of the Embedded Messaging pilot, data will also be available in Campaign Analytics. This gives you full visibility of your omnichannel performance, including inbox campaigns, in one place—no separate setup or switching between applications required.  Interested clients only need to apply to be in the Embedded Messaging pilot. 

View active pilots and early adopter programs here and contact your Customer Success Manager if you’re interested in joining any of the new pilots featured in this release. 

 Custom Chat Integration: Rich Communication Services (Early Adopter Care) 

A new custom Chat Messenger Integration is also coming to Early Adopter Care. As a modern industry standard for messaging supported by Google, RCS enables more dynamic and secure conversations than SMS or MMS. Begin setup using existing vendor integrations with Infobip, Sinch, or Vonage. Then, complete the configuration and create campaigns in SAP Engagement Cloud as you do in WhatsApp or LINE. Use rich cards to combine media and text into carousels, provide suggested replies or actions, or even load web pages directly within the channel for seamless engagement. For example, a retailer can send a branded RCS order update with rich cards that let customers track delivery, get support, or browse recommended products all directly within the message. 

Data, Integrations, and Governance 

Better data leads to better engagement. This release strengthens how you connect, manage, and activate customer data across your ecosystem. 

SAP CDP Audience Builder in Engagement Cloud (Generally Available) 

With the SAP Customer Data Platform Audience Builder embedded in SAP Engagement Cloud, users can now access full CDP segmentation capabilities and data directly—without switching systems. For instance, in just a few clicks, you can create a CDP audience of contacts from the utility industry who are interested in a specific product and have a high engagement score. The contacts will appear as a CDP segment type once created, ready for use in campaigns, personalization, and automation. 

“We chose SAP Engagement Cloud as the best technological match in our opinion because the tool fits perfectly into our system landscape with CDC and CDP and stands out with its intuitive, user-friendly handling.” 
 Philipp Hoehler. Product Owner Apps & Tools, DAW 

 Universal Schema Builder (Pilot) 

Universal Schema Builder allows you to use data from external databases and combine segmentation and personalization queries into a single schema. With this release, personalization fields can now be used independently without relying on the same USB segment data, giving you more flexibility in how you build and deliver personalized experiences. In addition, USB supports concurrent campaigns—meaning marketers can use the same Universal Schema across multiple campaigns simultaneously. This improves upon existing Relational Data functionalities, providing marketers with greater flexibility and reducing complexity when activating external data within SAP Engagement Cloud. 

Flexible External Contact ID Management (Generally Available) 

Flexible External Contact ID Management provides more robust options for managing and aligning external IDs across systems, including flexible mapping between your external system contact identifiers and SAP Engagement Cloud contact records. By enabling you to keep your native identifiers as a custom field in SAP Engagement Cloud, you preserve data quality and reduce the possibility of creating duplicate contacts across a complex system landscape. 

View Contacts in Segment Editor (Generally Available) 

Enhancements to View Contacts make it easier to inspect and understand individual contact records, with clearer visibility into key identity and engagement attributes. This improves usability for day-to-day contact management and better supports data hygiene and governance activities. 

Business Areas Enhancements 

This release also includes improvements to the Business Areas capability, which was introduced as part of Enterprise Edition to allow organizations with complex operational structures (e.g., multiple brands, regions, countries) to separate data, contacts, and assets and maintain necessary levels of governance and control. 

Support for Bounce Mail Management (Generally Available) 

Bounce Mail Management enhances how bounced emails are handled and tracked, enabling you with: 

  • Better classification and management of bounces 
  • Improved list hygiene 
  • Protection of sender reputation and deliverability 

With expanded support, you can now view and manage this information on a more granular level for each Business Area you have set up. 

ID Mapping Support for Standard Sales Data (Early Adopter) 

Map and resolve external Business Area identifiers to your internal Business Areas, ensuring incoming sales data automatically lands in the right place. This can be a 1:1 relationship or a Many:1 mapping. For instance, you can do this if you have regional business units (e.g., states) that you want to roll into a single Business Area (e.g., country) for campaign execution and reporting. 

Mapping Support in SAP CDP Connector (Generally Available) 

Enhanced mapping options in the SAP CDP Connector strengthen data synchronization between SAP CDP and SAP Engagement Cloud, ensuring contact profiles are synced to the correct Business Area. 

Default Business Area Enhancement (Generally Available) 

Rename and update the default Business Area to reflect your organization's operational structure, with clear UI indicators to help admins understand its role and restrictions. 

What’s Next? 

This release is another step toward the vision of the autonomous enterprise—a world where intelligent systems continuously learn, adapt, and act in real time to create better outcomes for practitioners, their customers, and the business.  

From AI embedded directly into campaign workflows, to a new brand-owned messaging channel that meets customers wherever they are, to deeper data connectivity across your SAP and external ecosystems, every update in this release is designed to help you do more with less friction. 

SAP Engagement Cloud exists to enable what is possible so that businesses can engage smarter, move faster, and ultimately deliver more meaningful customer experiences that grow revenue and loyalty. 

To explore all the innovations in this release and see them in action, visit the SAP Engagement Cloud product release page.

entry.title: Special Reserve Finland S/4 - table T095P -
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

we have a requirement from Finland where, in ECC, the depreciation areas are configured as shown in hardcopy 1.

The question particularly relates to depreciation  area 18, which posts the difference between 15 and 01, see hardcopy 2.

For this, the accounts are determined in AO90 via table T095P, see hardcopy 3. 

In S/4, there are no depreciation areas that are derived and can post.

Derived depreciation areas can be set up in S/4, but only for calculation purposes—such as depreciation area 18 in this example (see S/4 hardcopy 1–3).

Since we have been informed by the business department in Finland that this is a legal requirement, it would be helpful to understand how such a requirement can be implemented.
"However, under Finnish statutory reporting requirements, it is mandatory to present this difference in the financial statements (Kirjanpitoasetus | 1339/1997 | Legislation | Finlex):"

Apparently, there is no country-specific version for Finland available for this purpose, even if it is legally required.

How do other companies/groups implement the legal requirement in Finland?
Is there another best practice solution for this?

Does anyone have an idea?
Thank you very much.

entry.title: Protecting Your SAP Landscape: Addressing the SQL Authorization Bypass (CVE-2026-27681)
entry.content:
Introduction

Not every critical vulnerability announces itself. This one surfaced during a routine review of ABAP upload tooling, which is exactly why it had gone unnoticed for as long as it did.

For context, a CVSS 9.9 sits one step below full system compromise with zero user interaction required. Finding it inside infrastructure that most Basis teams consider routine was not what I expected. I ran through my findings twice before I was comfortable saying it out loud.

The Problem

What the Vulnerability Actually Does

This is not a classic SQL injection. An authenticated user, someone who already has a valid login to your system, can bypass internal security checks inside ABAP upload tooling and execute arbitrary SQL against your database. Full read, write, and delete access. Your firewall does nothing here. Your perimeter controls do nothing here. The attacker is already inside using tools that look completely normal.

Formal disclosure: CVE-2026-27681, SAP Note 3719353.

Affected components:

1) HANABPC: 810

2) BPC4HANA: 300

3) SAP_BW: Versions 750 through 816

Check This Before You Do Anything Else

Go look at who holds Activity 60 on S_GUI in your system right now.

Activity 60 is the Upload activity and it is the specific attack vector this vulnerability uses. In my experience it gets copied into standard end-user roles from old templates and sits there unaudited for years because it never caused a visible problem. Run your SUIM check. You may not like what you find.

Revoking Activity 60 from standard end-user roles is your immediate mitigation if you cannot patch right now. It does not fix the root cause but it closes the door this exploit walks through.

The Permanent Fix

Apply the corrections in SAP Note 3719353. These support packages deactivate the vulnerable code path entirely:

SAPK-75035INSAPBW

SAPK-81019INHANABPC

SAPK-30014INBPC4HANA

BW landscapes on versions 750 through 755 tend to lag the most on support package currency in my experience. If that is your situation, this goes to the top of your next maintenance window.

On Timing

Once a note at this severity level is public, the exploitation window shrinks fast. I have seen it go from weeks to days. Internal tooling exploits are particularly attractive to attackers because they blend in with normal system activity and rarely trigger alerts.

If emergency patching is not possible through your change management process, apply the S_GUI workaround today and document it as a formal compensating control. Your auditors will want to see it.

Conclusion

The vulnerabilities that worry me most are the ones sitting inside infrastructure nobody is watching because it has always worked fine. That is what this was. CVE-2026-27681 is a reminder that attack surfaces in SAP landscapes rarely look the way you expect them to.

Patch when you can. Restrict Activity 60 today if you cannot. And take a closer look at the parts of your landscape your team considers routine.

If you have already worked through remediation, drop a comment below. Particularly curious what turned up in your S_GUI review during the cleanup.

Thanks & Regards

HarshaTeja

entry.title: Creating a Custom F4 Help for Currency Fields in ABAP Using F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST
entry.content:
Introduction :

In SAP ABAP, F4 Help (Search Help) is used to help users select valid values instead of entering them manually. Sometimes, the standard search help does not meet business requirements, and a custom F4 Help needs to be created. In this example, we will create a custom F4 Help for a currency field using the function module F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST . The currency codes and descriptions are fetched from the TCURC and TCURT tables and displayed in a user-friendly selection list.

REPORT zva_search_help_parameter.

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* Selection Screen
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
SELECTION-SCREEN BEGIN OF BLOCK b1 WITH FRAME TITLE text-001.

  " Amount Parameter
  PARAMETERS : p_amount TYPE ktwrt,

               " Currency Parameter for Custom F4 Help
               p_wears  TYPE tcurc-waers.

SELECTION-SCREEN END OF BLOCK b1.

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* Custom F4 Help for Currency Field
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
AT SELECTION-SCREEN ON VALUE-REQUEST FOR p_wears.

  " Internal table to store selected value
  DATA: lt_values TYPE TABLE OF ddshretval.

  " Internal table for field information (optional)
  DATA: lt_fields TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF dfies.

  " Fetch Currency Key and Currency Description
  SELECT a~waers,
         b~ltext
    FROM tcurc AS a
    INNER JOIN tcurt AS b
      ON a~waers = b~waers
   WHERE b~spras = 'E'          " English Language Texts
    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_list).

  " Display Custom F4 Help Popup
  CALL FUNCTION 'F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST'
    EXPORTING
      retfield        = 'WAERS'     " Return Currency Key
      dynpprog        = sy-repid    " Current Program Name
      dynpnr          = sy-dynnr    " Current Screen Number
      dynprofield     = 'P_WEARS'   " Selection Screen Field
      value_org       = 'S'         " Structured Internal Table
    TABLES
      value_tab       = lt_list     " Data to Display in F4 Help
    EXCEPTIONS
      parameter_error = 1
      no_values_found = 2
      OTHERS          = 3.

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    MESSAGE 'No values found for Currency Search Help'
      TYPE 'I'.
  ENDIF.

Output : 

Conclusion :

Creating a custom F4 Help in ABAP improves the user experience by providing meaningful value selections. Using F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST , we can easily display data from internal tables and return the selected value to a screen field. This approach is useful when custom search logic or additional information needs to be displayed to users.

entry.title: Re: 2PD Provisioning for Doubtful receivable - Perform fu...
entry.content:
Hi Ahmed,

While I cannot look directly into your system, an analysis of the job log and standard YCOA design strongly suggests that the issue lies in your selection parameters rather than your account determination configuration.

Looking at the error F107: 104 Account determination not correct YCOA 0012401000 B03, the system is actively trying to use 12401000 as the source reconciliation account to look up the B03 rules. Because it cannot find it mapped as a source account, it returns a blank target account. This blank value is exactly what triggers the subsequent error G/L account is not defined...  (notice the two empty spaces where the target account number should be).

In the standard SAP Best Practices YCOA, 12401000 is typically defined as your Adjustment Account (Doubtful Receivables), not your base Trade Receivables reconciliation account. Check the pre-delivered automatic account determination screenshot below.

It appears you are running the F107 program on the adjustment account itself. The Further Valuation program is designed to evaluate open items on your standard customer reconciliation accounts (e.g., 12100000 or 12100100).

How to resolve this:

  1. Open the parameters of your F107 run.

  2. Ensure that you are selecting your standard Trade Receivables reconciliation accounts.

  3. Exclude the doubtful receivables/adjustment accounts (like 12401000) from the run selection.

Once F107 reads the open items from the correct trade receivables account, it will successfully find the B03 mapping and post the provision to 12401000 as the designated target.

Please let me know if my suggestion is right. If not, please share with me your automatic account determination configured in you system for the mentioned transaction key and the job parameters you have used.

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 02 - Content for common reuse types
Building an Agentic Enterprise AI App on SAP BTP: Lessons Learned
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

In this article, we share our experience and lessons learned while developing an MVP of an agentic AI application running on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP).

Our goal was to build an agent that supports users when creating projects in SAP. Some of you may be familiar with transactions such as CJ20N (Project Builder), CJ11 (Create WBS Elements), or CN21 (Create Networks). An SAP project consists of multiple elements, including project definitions, WBS elements, networks, activities, and activity-related materials. Based on this, we built an agent designed to guide users through project creation with the support of LLMs powered through SAP AI Core.

The following architecture illustrates the approach we followed to build the MVP. While developing the Project Builder Agent, we also used the publicly available OData V4 services from SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

 

Start with the APIs, not with AI

Before starting to develop any ideas regarding LLMs, Agentic Frameworks or code related to the interaction with AI, it was and is (from our point of view) particularly important to first start dealing with the APIs you want to use within your project. Because we think no matter which tech stack and which tools you ise along the development of your use case, you will work with APIs / OData services within your tools. For that you need to exactly know how the APIs are working (e.g. which payloads are expected + accepted or structure of data). This took the longest time while developing the MVP and for us it was also the most critical part.

Since a project in SAP consists of several elements (mentioned above) we created a project via APIs step by step. We started by posting just the project base (shell) and then slowly added WBS Elements (root and child elements) to the project, then Networks and Activities to the given WBS elements. While playing around with the API we got to know the project structure in SAP and how exactly the API works, especially when the Business Object with which you deal consists of multiple sub dataobjects. This "testing" and "playing" with the APIs allowed us to build the payload structure which we used in a later step for our AI-based user interaction.

For us it was very helpful to do it that way. While developing MVPs before, we immediately started developing the AI part. Calling LLMs, Agents and implemented tools. Then we ran into problems since we hadn't touched the API part and ran into several issues because we had not spent much time thinking about the most important part: the data source and structure of the tool the Agent uses.

That's why we decided to do it the other way around this time, and it sped up development enormously. Also, we knew all the pitfalls (at certain inputs in the payload, SAP only accepts a certain length of a string, or some other detail related to the hierarchy of project elements). Once we got to know them, we immediately implemented rules that the Agent can follow and can post a successful payload to SAP without running into weird errors (which usually end in long debug sessions most of the time).

Fixed Payload Structure for Fast Prototyping

Also for an MVP it was crucial to start with a fixed payload structure. Meaning for our Project Builder Agent case: a particular number of WBS Elements, Activities and Networks. While doing this, the complexity was reduced drastically and allowed us fast prototyping. With that “fixed” payload we could focus on the AI-generated content (descriptions and content for project definitions, activities, networks and WBS elements) and the user interaction flow.

We also decided to use a more “static” approach for the MVP and have chosen LangGraph as an orchestrator. Here is a high-level overview of the LangGraph nodes:

 

Faced Problems During Development

  • Agent Ambiguity and Compound Intents & Loops: When I wanted to post a data set, I coded that node of the agent to react to something like “Yes, post.” But if the user also writes “please post” and adds “but also change the description to X”, it broke. The binary confirm/reject logic had no concept of compound intents. The result: either silent intent loss or robotic fallbacks (“Please confirm with ‘yes, post please’”).

 

 

  • Robotic Responses feel awkward: Users felt they were talking to a form, not an assistant. Especially while developing the Langgraph flow we needed to make sure to route generic questions (which are out of the decision range of a Langgraph based Agent) to route directly to the LLM.

 

(In the screenshots above some sampels of the problems we have faced. Long debuggung until getting the userflow somehow interactve 🤖😂)

Improvements & Next Steps

While creating Agents / Projects like this, we also came up with a huge improvement list which we want to implement in the future. Here are some key ideas:

  • Dynamic Payload Building: Let the AI / the Agent decide how many Project Elements the payload should have
  • Project Based Analytics: Since a project is often connected to other well-known SAP Elements like Cost Center or Materials it would be interesting to Analyze the material consumption of certain projects or the time and cost they need usually
  • Rebuild to an MCP server: Rebuild the deployed python service as MCP server to make the tools available to Joule, Claude or MS CoPilot
  • Move from Langgraph to a truly agentic setup: Make use of known LLM SDKs to create one Agent per Task (Like creating, validating and posting the Payload) as well as an orchestrator

Aspects to consider

Even though our list of improvements is long, there are a few things which should be considered implementing AI Agent / embedded AI in Enterprise Applications:

  • Token Consumption: When shifting to a Multi Agent Approach (e.g. away from Langgraph) you need to consider that more Tokens will be consumed. Unless you set limited guardrails for your Agentic System a lot of tokens can be consumed for a very simple and clear intent
  • Latency: Always think about the user experience and the time the LLM answer needs to come back to the application. Keep in mind to make use of Streaming APIs as well to improve user experience -> https://help.sap.com/doc/generative-ai-hub-sdk/CLOUD/en-US/_reference/streaming.html

Last but not least, we would be interested to know how you build Enterprise AI Applications (architecture wise) and how you deal with upcoming problems. Please let us know in the comments below.

entry.title: Re: I need help to make Adobe Livecycle Designer 11 work...
entry.content:
I finally got it to work.

I uninstalled everything, but the uninstallation does not clean up properly.

So I ran a registry cleaner (CC cleaner) to help me find all remaining references to SAPgui and Adobe Livecycle designer.
I manually deleted all these registry entries and if that lead to empty "folders", I also deleted those.

Then I re-installed SAPgui 8.00 patch 15 and Adobe livecycle designer is version 11.0.9.20260128.1.71.

And now it works.

I don't know why the installation failed in the first place, but all my previous attempts to re-install seems to fail because the un-install scripts did not properly clean the registry.

Best regards
Thomas Madsen Nielsen

entry.title: Version Management in SAP GUI
entry.content:
Introduction 

The blog provides step by step guide on Version Management of Report programs, Classes, Function Modules and Data dictionary objects. 

In this blog, we will cover 

  • What is Version Management 
  • How to compare the versions 
  • How to revert the code changes to previous versions 
  • How to display only code changed part. 

Version Management 

  • Version Management refers to the process of maintaining and controlling different versions of SAP GUI (Graphical User Interface) and related components used to access SAP systems. 
  • It ensures that users are running the correct and compatible version of SAP GUI when logging into SAP systems. 

Comparing the versions 

Execute SE38 (ABAP Editor) and provide the Report program name and then navigate through the menu option Utilities -> Versions -> Version Management 

You can find the versions 

  1.  Active Version (Current) 
  • Status: Green square  
  • Version: active 
  • Date/Time: 29.05.2026 at 11:37:41 
  • Author: S23NEW27 

This is the current working version of your program. 
 This is what runs when you execute the program. 

  1.  Older Saved Version 
  • Status: Yellow triangle 
  • Version: 1 
  • Cat.: U (User version) 
  • Date/Time: 29.05.2026 at 11:37:29 
  • Author: S23NEW27 

This is a previous version saved automatically by SAP when changes were made. 

To compare the versions select the versions then click on compare icon 

We can observe the changes in versions (code changed). 

Retrieve the code changes to previous version 

Execute SE38 (ABAP Editor) and provide the Report program name and then navigate through the menu option Utilities -> Versions -> Version Management 

Select the previous version 1 and then click on Retrieve button in Application tool bar 

It gives the popup whether we want to overwrite the previous version. 

Click on continue icon 

Now we need to active the program to show as active version as it’s in modify status 

Once we have activated the program we can observe that a new version is generated 

now compare version active and 2 

The changed code is reverted to previous  

Need to display only the Code changed places while comparing the versions 

Click on Settings 

Select radio button Display differences only and then click on continue icon 

Now we can observe only the differences in code or changes in code. 

 

Conclusion 

Version management in SAP GUI provides a structured way to monitor and control changes made to system objects over time. It enhances reliability by allowing users to track modifications, compare versions, and revert to earlier states when needed. This ensures better system stability, supports compliance requirements, and improves overall development efficiency.
